title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Significant Increase in CA LCFS Type 1 Volumes with Moderate Price Drop; Non-Zero Volumes and Prices Witness Dip
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(21st April â€“ 27th April)
  • A total of 552,368 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a rise of 75.15% week-over-week (WoW), while all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 53.30% WoW as 1,039,822 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $57.82/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 0.55% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 7.3% in price, reaching $58.53/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price fell by 0.83% WoW; on 28th April, the spot price stood at $58.5/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $63.5/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 1.61% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 3.09% WoW each.
cCarbonâ€™s Market View CARBâ€™s latest LCFS package, with the comment period closing on April 21st, received 81 submissions from diverse stakeholders, with the overall sentiment leaning in favor of the proposed changes. Stakeholder Feedback reveals broad support for increasing station capacity, removing credit caps, and making hydrogen infrastructure more flexible.Â Feedback on CARBâ€™s Land Use Change (LUC) methodology shows general support for ongoing refinement, with some stakeholders offering to contribute data to improve carbon intensity modelling. Biofuels associations continue to press against restrictions pertaining to grain based biofuels, referring to the previous comment period. For more details on the feedback received, give our analysis on the comments a read. The week also witnessed the EPAâ€™s approval of the nationwide summer sale of E15. [caption id="attachment_596071" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 0.55% WoW fall, decreasing from $58.14 to $57.82 this week, while all non-zero credit prices decreased by 7.30% WoW, from $63.14 to $58.53. Type 1 trades saw 552,368 credits exchanged, reflecting a rise of 75.1% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a fall of 53.3% to reach 1,039,822 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_596073" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 0.83% WoW, reaching $57.65/MT. On 28th April, the average spot price was $58.50/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $63.50/MT.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_596075" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 66 trades were reported in the market, with 8 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $77.00 for two Type 2 transactions at volumes of 2,092 and 2,908 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $30.11 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at a volume of 27 credits. The smallest volume of transfer was for 6 credits of the Type 1 category, undertaken for a price of $60.00. The largest volume transfers of 160,000 credits of the Type 1 category was undertaken for a price of $58.00.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_596076" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] Average (Type 1) spot price reached $58.24 as of 27th April, lower than the Average (Type 1) spot prices for March, which stood at $58.44. With last weekâ€™s trading, volumes rose to 8.57 MMT this month, cementing the monthâ€™s volumes as the highest in the Programâ€™s history.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_596077" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 21st April â€“ 27th April, total VPT recorded a 19.00% fall WoW, with a fall in all non-zero prices.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices witnessed a fall of 1.61% WoW. D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 3.09% WoW each. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

CARB Receives Over 80 Public Comments on Third LCFS 15-Day Package Â CARB has received 81 comments from biofuel groups, OEMs, charging and refueling infrastructure providers and other stakeholders on its third 15-day Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) package. The public comment period concluded on April 21st. cCarbon will soon publish a comprehensive analysis of the comments received. Amended Version of AB30 Pushed Forward to Committee to Expedite Authorization of E15 Sale in California An amended version of AB30 has been pushed forward to Committee to expedite the authorization of E15 sale in California. The bill could allow gasoline with 10.5% to 15% ethanol to be sold in California, despite current restrictions. This would be a temporary measure until the California Environmental Policy Council reviews the blends and the state board either sets formal fuel standards or confirms that such standards canâ€™t meet legal requirements. Supreme Court Hears Case on California's Vehicle Emissions EPA Waiver The Supreme Court heard oral argument in Diamond Alternative Energy, LLC v. Environmental Protection Agency, a case examining whether fuel companies may challenge an EPA waiver that allows California to set its own auto emissions standards. The companies argued for their right to sue the EPA, explaining their business interests were harmed by the waiver. The U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit ruled that the fuel companies lacked standing, and this appeal followed. ABFA To EPA: U.S. Environmental Protection Agency Must Issue 2026 RVOs Reflecting Strong Domestic Biofuel Production CapacityÂ The Advanced Biofuels Association (ABFA) met with officials in the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to convey the vital importance of domestic biofuel production to the Trump-Vance administrationâ€™s energy dominance policy agenda. Following a recent report produced by Lipow Oil Associates, which indicated in 2024 U.S. producers made nearly 4.9 billion gallons of advanced biofuels and will have capacity in 2025 to make up to 7.2 billion gallons, ABFA urged EPA to reflect the strength of Americaâ€™s domestic biofuel production capacity by increasing its 2026 Renewable Volume Obligations (RVOs) to 5.75 billion gallons. Oregon DEQ Proposes to Delay the CFP 2024 Annual Report Deadline Following CyberattackÂ Oregon DEQ is proposing to delay the 2024 Annual Report Deadline to Friday, May 30, 2025 due to the cyberattack and extended outage of the Oregon Fuels Reporting System. On Wednesday April 9, DEQ was struck with a cyberattack that crippled agency systems, including the Oregon Fuels Reporting System that is used by fuel suppliers to file their reporting for the CFP and Greenhouse Gas Reporting Program, generate and trade Clean Fuels Program credits, and demonstrate compliance with the Clean Fuels Program. California Awards $500 Million in Funding for 1,000 Zero-emission School Buses The State of California has awarded $500 million for educational agencies to buy zero-emission school buses and chargers. The Zero-Emissions School Bus and Infrastructure (ZESBI) project has selected 133 educational agencies to receive 1,000 zero-emission school buses and related charging infrastructure to school districts and other local educational entities. The incentive program is a collaboration between the California Air Resources Board (CARB) and the California Energy Commission (CEC), and is administered by CALSTART, a nonprofit transportation organization.
